topic: A student learns their thesis advisor is leaving the university
raw_output:
[story 1]

The office door clicked shut behind Dr. Albright, leaving a hollow echo in the otherwise silent faculty lounge. I stared at the framed photo on his desk—a smiling picture taken at a departmental picnic—and the edges of my vision blurred. He was leaving. The news had landed like a physical blow, sharp and cold, right in the center of my chest. I picked up my pen, the cheap plastic feeling slick and alien in my hand. Every meticulously researched paragraph, every late night spent wrestling with my methodology, suddenly seemed tainted, a monument to a relationship that was about to dissolve. I began to furiously highlight sections of my draft, not to improve the argument, but as if I could somehow scrub away the entire endeavor. My jaw tightened until my molars ached. I needed to do something, anything, to counteract the sudden, overwhelming sense of inadequacy that flooded my system.

[story 2]

"So, you're really moving on?" I managed, my voice sounding thin and reedy even to my own ears. I kept my gaze fixed on the chipped paint of the windowsill, avoiding the direct line of sight to the empty chair where Professor Davies usually sat. The air in the hallway felt thick, suffocating. I could feel a tremor starting in my hands, a slight vibration that made gripping my backpack straps feel like a desperate anchor. It was my fault, wasn't it? For not asking enough questions, for letting the momentum slip, for not being sharp enough to keep his attention. A wave of heat washed over my cheeks, and I instinctively pulled my collar up, a futile attempt to hide the flush. I walked toward the exit, my steps dragging, each footfall a heavy acknowledgment of the unfinished work I was leaving behind, a silence that screamed louder than any spoken apology.

[story 3]

The fluorescent lights of the library hummed with an oppressive monotony that mirrored the churning in my stomach. I reread the final chapter of my dissertation proposal, the words swimming before my eyes—words that felt suddenly meaningless, hollowed out by the knowledge that my primary guide was departing. I pressed my forehead against the cool surface of the desk, trying to absorb some sense of permanence from the wood grain. A sudden, sharp intake of breath escaped me; my shoulders hitched upward, rigid with a tension I couldn't seem to release. I started pacing the narrow aisle between the stacks, my movements jerky and uncoordinated. Each turn of my head brought me face-to-face with the spines of other books, each title mocking my stalled progress. I felt a profound sense of dereliction, as if I had failed the very purpose of my academic journey, and the shame settled over me like a heavy, suffocating shroud.
